author: Roald Dahl
passage: 'Just because I is a giant, you think I is a man-gobbling cannybull!' he shouted. 'You is about right! Giants is all cannybully and murderful! And they does gobble up human beans!' … 'The human bean is not a vegetable.' 'Oh, but the bean is a vegetable,' Sophie said. 'Not the human bean,' the Giant said. 'The human bean has two legs and a vegetable has no legs at all.' Sophie didn't argue any more. The last thing she wanted to do was to make the Giant cross. 'The human bean,' the Giant went on, 'is coming in dillions of different flavours. For instance, human beans from Wales is tasting very whooshey of fish.' 'You means whales,' Sophie said. 'Wales is something quite different.' 'Wales is whales,' the Giant said. 'Don't gobblefunk around with words.'
socratic_questions: The BFG says 'Wales is whales' — they sound the same but mean different things. What do we call words that sound identical but have different meanings?
Why does Dahl have the BFG confuse 'human bean' with 'human being'? Is the BFG really stupid, or is he doing something else?
Can you find three other invented words the BFG uses in this passage, and work out what they really mean from the context?
hook: Meet the BFG — a giant who gets words completely muddled up, but somehow that makes him funnier and wiser than the giants who speak properly.

author: Roald Dahl
passage: 'Yesterday,' he said, 'we was not believing in giants, was we? Today we is not believing in snozzcumbers. Just because we happen not to have actually seen something with our own two little winkles, we think it is not existing.' … 'Words,' he said, 'is oh such a twitch-tickling problem to me all my life. So you must simply try to be patient and stop squibbling.' … 'I is speaking the most terrible wigglish.' 'I think you speak beautifully,' Sophie said. 'You do?' cried the BFG, suddenly brightening. 'You really do?' 'Simply beautifully,' Sophie repeated. 'Well, that is the nicest present anybody is ever giving me in my whole life!' cried the BFG.
socratic_questions: The BFG says we shouldn't disbelieve things just because we haven't seen them. Can you think of something you believe in that you've never directly seen?
Sophie says the BFG speaks beautifully even though he makes lots of mistakes. Do you think she's being kind or honest — or both? Does it matter which?
What do you think the BFG means when he says 'what I mean and what I say is two different things'? Has that ever happened to you?
hook: The BFG has never been to school and muddles up his words terribly — but he has some of the wisest ideas in the whole book.

author: Roald Dahl
passage: 'Every dream in the world is making a different sort of buzzy-hum music. And these grand swashboggling ears of mine is able to read that music.' … 'The matter with human beans,' the BFG went on, 'is that they is absolutely refusing to believe in anything unless they is actually seeing it right in front of their own schnozzles. Of course quogwinkles is existing. I is meeting them oftenly. I is even chittering to them.' … Sophie peered into the jar and there, sure enough, she saw the faint translucent outline of something about the size of a hen's egg. There was just a touch of colour in it, a pale sea-green, soft and shimmering and very beautiful. There it lay, this small oblong sea-green jellyish thing, at the bottom of the jar, quite peaceful, but pulsing gently, the whole of it moving in and out ever so slightly, as though it were breathing.
socratic_questions: Dahl describes the dream as 'pulsing gently, moving in and out as though it were breathing' — he makes an abstract thing feel alive. What technique is he using, and can you describe something invisible in the same way?
The BFG says humans only believe things they can see — but he believes in quogwinkles. Who do you think is being more scientific, Sophie or the BFG?
If you could bottle one of your dreams, what would it look like? What colour, shape, and movement would it have?
hook: The BFG keeps thousands of dreams in glass jars on his shelves — and today Sophie gets to see one for the very first time.

author: Roald Dahl
passage: It's a funny thing about mothers and fathers. Even when their own child is the most disgusting little blister you could ever imagine, they still think that he or she is wonderful. Some parents go further. They become so blinded by adoration they manage to convince themselves their child has qualities of genius. Well, there is nothing very wrong with all this. It's the way of the world. It is only when the parents begin telling us about the brilliance of their own revolting offspring, that we start shouting, 'Bring us a basin! We're going to be sick!' … Occasionally one comes across parents who take the opposite line, who show no interest at all in their children, and these of course are far worse than the doting ones. Mr and Mrs Wormwood were two such parents.
socratic_questions: Why does Dahl say parents who ignore their children are 'far worse' than parents who think their children are geniuses? Do you agree?
Dahl is talking directly to the reader here, as if he's having a conversation. What effect does that create — does it make you trust him more or less as a narrator?
Dahl uses the word 'adoration' — what does that mean, and is it always a bad thing to adore someone?
hook: Before we even meet Matilda, Roald Dahl stops to give his very strong opinions about parents — and they're not very flattering.

author: Roald Dahl
passage: Now most head teachers are chosen because they possess a number of fine qualities. They understand children and they have the children's best interests at heart. They are sympathetic. They are fair and they are deeply interested in education. Miss Trunchbull possessed none of these qualities and how she ever got her present job was a mystery. She was above all a most formidable female. She had once been a famous athlete, and even now the muscles were still clearly in evidence. You could see them in the bull-neck, in the big shoulders, in the thick arms, in the sinewy wrists and in the powerful legs. Looking at her, you got the feeling that this was someone who could bend iron bars and tear telephone directories in half.
socratic_questions: Dahl lists good qualities that most headteachers have, then says Miss Trunchbull had none of them. Why does he use that technique — starting with the opposite?
Find three physical details Dahl uses to show what Miss Trunchbull's character is like. How does a person's body language or appearance tell us things about their personality?
Dahl says 'how she ever got her present job was a mystery.' Why does he phrase it that way instead of just saying she was bad at her job?
hook: Miss Trunchbull is one of the most terrifying teachers in all of children's fiction — let's look at exactly how Dahl makes her so frightening with just his words.

author: Norton Juster
passage: There was once a boy named Milo who didn't know what to do with himself—not just sometimes, but always. When he was in school he longed to be out, and when he was out he longed to be in. On the way he thought about coming home, and coming home he thought about going. Wherever he was he wished he were somewhere else, and when he got there he wondered why he'd bothered. Nothing really interested him—least of all the things that should have. 'It seems to me that almost everything is a waste of time,' he remarked one day as he walked dejectedly home from school. 'I can't see the point in learning to solve useless problems, or subtracting turnips from turnips, or knowing where Ethiopia is or how to spell February.'
socratic_questions: Milo can never enjoy where he is because he's always thinking about somewhere else. Have you ever felt like that? What do you think causes it?
Milo says learning is 'a waste of time.' By the end of the book, do you think he still believes this? What might have changed his mind?
Juster says Milo was bored by things 'least of all the things that should have' interested him. Why does boredom often hit the things we're supposed to care about?
hook: This book begins with one of the most relatable openings in children's literature — a boy who is bored by absolutely everything.

author: Norton Juster
passage: 'I'm the Whether Man, not the Weather Man, for after all it's more important to know whether there will be weather than what the weather will be.' … 'What kind of a place is Expectations?' inquired Milo. 'Good question, good question,' he exclaimed. 'Expectations is the place you must always go to before you get to where you're going. Of course, some people never go beyond Expectations, but my job is to hurry them along whether they like it or not.' … 'Expect everything, I always say, and the unexpected never happens.'
socratic_questions: 'Whether' and 'weather' sound the same but mean different things — what do we call this? Can you invent a character called the 'Which Man' or the 'Knot Man' using the same idea?
The Whether Man says 'expect everything and the unexpected never happens.' Is that actually good advice? What would happen if you tried to apply it in real life?
Juster invented a land called Expectations. If you were designing a map for this book, what other lands would you put next to it?
hook: Before Milo can get anywhere in this magical land, he has to pass through Expectations — and its very confused guardian has a lot of advice about weather.

author: Norton Juster
passage: 'Do all those words mean the same thing?' gasped Milo. 'Of course.' 'Certainly.' 'Precisely.' 'Exactly.' 'Yes,' they replied in order. 'Well, then,' said Milo, not understanding why each one said the same thing in a slightly different way, 'wouldn't it be simpler to use just one? It would certainly make more sense.' 'Nonsense.' 'Ridiculous.' 'Fantastic.' 'Absurd.' 'Bosh,' they chorused again … 'We're not interested in making sense; it's not our job,' scolded the first. 'Besides,' explained the second, 'one word is as good as another—so why not use them all?' 'Then you don't have to choose which one is right,' advised the third. 'Besides,' sighed the fourth, 'if one is right, then ten are ten times as right.'
socratic_questions: The Duke of Definition says 'if one word is right, then ten are ten times as right.' Is that actually true? What's wrong with that logic?
Do synonyms really mean exactly the same thing? Think of 'happy,' 'joyful,' 'content,' and 'ecstatic' — what are the differences?
Why might Juster have made the king's advisers have titles like 'The Count of Connotation' — what does connotation mean, and why is it important?
hook: In Dictionopolis, words grow on trees and five royal ministers each say the same thing using different words — which gets Milo into an argument about whether synonyms are really identical.

author: Roald Dahl
passage: In fairy-tales, witches always wear silly black hats and black cloaks, and they ride on broomsticks. But this is not a fairy-tale. This is about REAL WITCHES. The most important thing you should know about REAL WITCHES is this. Listen very carefully. Never forget what is coming next. REAL WITCHES dress in ordinary clothes and look very much like ordinary women. They live in ordinary houses and they work in ORDINARY JOBS. That is why they are so hard to catch. A REAL WITCH hates children with a red-hot sizzling hatred that is more sizzling and red-hot than any hatred you could possibly imagine. A REAL WITCH spends all her time plotting to get rid of the children in her particular territory.
socratic_questions: Dahl opens by saying 'this is not a fairy-tale' — why does he do that, and do you believe him? What's the difference between a scary fairy-tale and a scary realistic story?
Dahl uses capital letters and repetition ('REAL WITCHES', 'ordinary') to create a particular effect. Read it aloud — what feeling does he create?
He says witches are hard to catch 'because they look ordinary.' Can you think of any real-world things that are dangerous precisely because they seem normal?
hook: Roald Dahl wants you to be genuinely scared right from page one — and his technique for doing it is surprising.

author: Roald Dahl
chapter: Chapter 3 — How to Recognise a Witch
passage: 'In the first place,' she said, 'a REAL WITCH is certain always to be wearing gloves when you meet her.' 'Surely not always,' I said. 'What about in the summer when it's hot?' 'Even in the summer,' my grandmother said. 'She has to. Do you want to know why?' … 'The second thing to remember is that a REAL WITCH is always bald.' 'Bald?' I said. 'Bald as a boiled egg.' … 'A REAL WITCH always wears a wig to hide her baldness. And it is almost impossible to tell a really first-class wig from ordinary hair unless you give it a pull to see if it comes off.' 'Then that's what I'll have to do,' I said. 'Don't be foolish,' my grandmother said. 'You can't go round pulling at the hair of every lady you meet, even if she is wearing gloves.'
socratic_questions: Grandmother gives clues that might fit many ordinary people (wearing gloves, wearing a wig). Why does she say these clues are useless on their own, and what's the better strategy?
This is a bit like detective work — scientists also look for multiple overlapping clues to identify something. Can you think of a problem where one clue alone isn't enough?

Dahl makes the boy ask exactly the right questions to move the story forward. Notice how each answer leads to the next question. Try doing this yourself: ask a question, give an answer, then ask 'but what about…?'
hook: Grandmother knows how to spot a real witch — but her clues have a problem. Let's investigate whether her evidence would actually hold up.

book: Charlie and the Great Glass Elevator
author: Roald Dahl
chapter: Chapter 1 — Mr Wonka Goes Too Far
passage: 'What in the world keeps this crazy thing up in the air?' croaked Grandma Josephine. 'Madam,' said Mr Wonka, 'it is not a lift any longer. Lifts only go up and down inside buildings. But now that it has taken us up into the sky, it has become an ELEVATOR.' … 'These skyhooks,' said Grandma Josephine. 'I assume one end is hooked on to this contraption we're riding in. Right?' 'Right,' said Mr Wonka. 'What's the other end hooked on to?' said Grandma Josephine. 'Every day,' said Mr Wonka, 'I get deafer and deafer. Remind me, please, to call up my ear doctor the moment we get back.' 'Charlie,' said Grandma Josephine. 'I don't think I trust this gentleman very much.'
socratic_questions: Mr Wonka pretends to go deaf to avoid answering an awkward question. Is this funny or rude — or both? What does it tell us about his character?
Mr Wonka says the elevator is held up by 'skyhooks.' Obviously that's not real — but what actually keeps aeroplanes and rockets in the air?
Grandma Josephine says she doesn't trust Mr Wonka. Based on this conversation, is she being sensible or too suspicious?
hook: Mr Wonka is in the Great Glass Elevator thousands of feet above the Earth, and when someone asks a very reasonable question about what's holding it up, he has a very Wonka-ish answer.

author: David Almond
passage: I found him in the garage on a Sunday afternoon. It was the day after we moved into Falconer Road. The winter was ending. Mum had said we'd be moving just in time for the spring. Nobody else was there. Just me. The others were inside the house with Doctor Death, worrying about the baby. He was lying there in the darkness behind the tea chests, in the dust and dirt. It was as if he'd been there forever. He was filthy and pale and dried out and I thought he was dead. I couldn't have been more wrong. I'd soon begin to see the truth about him, that there'd never been another creature like him in the world.
socratic_questions: Almond gives us three mysteries in one paragraph: who is 'Doctor Death,' who is 'the baby,' and who is 'him' in the garage. Why does a writer introduce mysteries like this rather than explaining everything?
The narrator says 'I thought he was dead. I couldn't have been more wrong.' This is called foreshadowing. What emotion does it create in you as a reader?
The writing is very short and simple — short sentences, simple words. Compare it to Roald Dahl's style. Which do you think creates more atmosphere here, and why?
hook: One sentence: 'I found him in the garage on a Sunday afternoon.' That's all it takes for David Almond to make you desperately want to know what happens next.

author: David Almond
passage: I tiptoed further in and felt spider webs breaking on my brow. Everything was packed in tight — ancient furniture, kitchen units, rolled-up carpets, pipes and crates and planks. I kept ducking down under the hosepipes and ropes and kitbags that hung from the roof. More cobwebs snapped on my clothes and skin. The floor was broken and crumbly. I opened a cupboard an inch, shone the torch in and saw a million woodlice scattering away. I peered down into a great stone jar and saw the bones of some little animal that had died in there. Dead bluebottles were everywhere. There were ancient newspapers and magazines. I shone the torch on to one and saw that it came from nearly fifty years ago.
socratic_questions: Almond uses lots of touch-based details — 'webs breaking on my brow,' 'cobwebs snapped on my skin.' Why focus on touch rather than sight or smell here?
He describes the garage as holding things from 'nearly fifty years ago.' Why does the age of objects matter to the atmosphere of this scene?
Write your own three-sentence description of a scary or mysterious place using only things your senses can detect — no saying 'it was scary' or 'it felt creepy.'
hook: Michael is creeping through a dark, crumbling garage full of old things — and David Almond shows us every cobweb, every dead bluebottle, every crumbly brick.

author: Steven D. Levitt and Stephen J. Dubner
chapter: Chapter 1 — What Do Schoolteachers and Sumo Wrestlers Have in Common?
passage: After the fine was enacted, the number of late pickups promptly went… up. Before long there were twenty late pickups per week, more than double the original average. The incentive had plainly backfired. Economics is, at root, the study of incentives: how people get what they want, or need, especially when other people want or need the same thing. … What was wrong with the incentive at the Israeli day-care centers? You have probably already guessed that the $3 fine was simply too small. But there was another problem. It substituted an economic incentive (the $3 penalty) for a moral incentive (the guilt that parents were supposed to feel when they came late). For just a few dollars each day, parents could buy off their guilt.
socratic_questions: The fine was supposed to reduce late pickups but doubled them instead. Why do you think the fine had the opposite effect?
The authors say the fine replaced a 'moral incentive' with an 'economic' one. What's the difference — can you think of another example where paying for something changed how people felt about it?
If you were running the day-care centre, what would you try instead? What incentive might work better than a fine?
hook: A day-care centre in Israel tried fining parents who picked up their children late — and something completely unexpected happened to the number of late pickups.

author: Steven D. Levitt and Stephen J. Dubner
chapter: Introduction — The Hidden Side of Everything
passage: The conventional wisdom is often wrong. Crime dropped in the 1990s. The smart money was plainly on the criminals — all the experts predicted a crime wave. And then, instead of going up and up and up, crime began to fall. And fall and fall and fall some more. The crime drop was startling in several respects. It was ubiquitous, with every category of crime falling in every part of the country. It was persistent, with incremental decreases year after year. And it was entirely unanticipated — especially by the very experts who had been predicting the opposite. The magnitude of the reversal was astounding. The teenage murder rate, instead of rising 100 percent or even 15 percent as James Alan Fox had warned, fell more than 50 percent within five years.
socratic_questions: The experts predicted crime would get much worse, but the opposite happened. Does this mean experts are useless, or does it mean prediction is genuinely hard? What's the difference?
Levitt says the drop was 'ubiquitous' — what does that word mean, and why is it important that crime fell everywhere, not just in one city?
The authors say the real cause of the crime drop surprised everyone and was something that happened twenty years earlier. What might explain a 20-year delay between a cause and its effect?
hook: In the 1990s, every expert predicted a massive crime wave was about to hit America. What actually happened was the exact opposite — and the real reason was something nobody had expected.

author: Steven D. Levitt and Stephen J. Dubner
chapter: Introduction — The Hidden Side of Everything
passage: A real-estate agent is a different breed of expert, but she is every bit the expert. That is, she knows her field far better than the layman on whose behalf she is acting. She is better informed about the house's value, the state of the housing market, even the buyer's frame of mind. You depend on her for this information. That, in fact, is why you hired an expert. As the world has grown more specialized, countless such experts have made themselves similarly indispensable. Doctors, lawyers, contractors, stockbrokers — an expert will exploit your ignorance if his interests run counter to yours. Which is not to say that experts are bad people. It is, however, to say that they are people — and people respond to incentives.
socratic_questions: Levitt says experts 'will exploit your ignorance if their interests run counter to yours.' Can you think of a real example where this might happen?
He's not saying experts are evil — just that they respond to incentives. What incentives does a doctor have? Are they always the same as yours?
How do you decide when to trust an expert? What questions could you ask to work out whether an expert's advice is in your interest or theirs?
hook: You hire experts because they know things you don't — but Levitt has a disturbing observation about what happens when the expert's interests are different from yours.

author: Daniel Kahneman
chapter: Chapter 1 — The Characters of the Story
passage: Your experience as you look at the woman's face seamlessly combines what we normally call seeing and intuitive thinking. As surely and quickly as you saw that the young woman's hair is dark, you knew she is angry. Furthermore, what you saw extended into the future. You sensed that this woman is about to say some very unkind words, probably in a loud and strident voice. A premonition of what she was going to do next came to mind automatically and effortlessly. You did not intend to assess her mood or to anticipate what she might do, and your reaction to the picture did not have the feel of something you did. It just happened to you. It was an instance of fast thinking. Now look at the following problem: 17 × 24. You knew immediately that this is a multiplication problem, and probably knew that you could solve it — but a precise solution did not come to mind.
socratic_questions: Kahneman says reading an angry face 'just happened' to you — you didn't choose to do it. Can you think of other things your brain does automatically, without you deciding to?
Why is 17 × 24 different from reading a face? What makes it require effort when the angry face didn't?
Kahneman calls these System 1 (fast, automatic) and System 2 (slow, effortful). Which system do you use when you're learning something new versus when you already know it well?
hook: Your brain has two very different ways of thinking — one that works like lightning without effort, and one that's more like doing homework. Kahneman shows you both in action at the same moment.

author: Daniel Kahneman
chapter: A Machine for Jumping to Conclusions
passage: Jumping to conclusions is efficient if the conclusions are likely to be correct and the costs of an occasional mistake acceptable, and if the jump saves much time and effort. Jumping to conclusions is risky when the situation is unfamiliar, the stakes are high, and there is no time to collect more information. … When uncertain, System 1 bets on an answer, and the bets are guided by experience. … System 1 does not keep track of alternatives that it rejects, or even of the fact that there were alternatives. Conscious doubt is not in the repertoire of System 1; it requires maintaining incompatible interpretations in mind at the same time, which demands mental effort. Uncertainty and doubt are the domain of System 2.
socratic_questions: Kahneman says jumping to conclusions is sometimes efficient and sometimes dangerous. What's the key question you should ask yourself before trusting your first instinct?
'Conscious doubt requires maintaining incompatible interpretations in mind at the same time.' Can you think of a question where you genuinely held two opposite answers at once?
Your brain automatically hides the alternatives it rejected. Why might this be a problem when someone is very sure they are right about something?
hook: Your brain is constantly making snap judgements and hiding the alternatives it rejected — Kahneman explains why this is usually useful but sometimes leads to confident mistakes.

author: Daniel Kahneman
passage: In the unlikely event of this book being made into a film, System 2 would be a supporting character who believes herself to be the hero. The defining feature of System 2, in this story, is that its operations are effortful, and one of its main characteristics is laziness, a reluctance to invest more effort than is strictly necessary. As a consequence, the thoughts and actions that System 2 believes it has chosen are often guided by the figure at the centre of the story, System 1. … It is normally easy and actually quite pleasant to walk and think at the same time, but at the extremes these activities appear to compete for the limited resources of System 2. You can confirm this claim by a simple experiment. While walking comfortably with a friend, ask him to compute 23 × 78 in his head, and to do so immediately. He will almost certainly stop in his tracks.
socratic_questions: Kahneman says System 2 'believes herself to be the hero.' What does he mean by that — do you feel in control of your own thinking, even when you might not be?
Try the walking-and-multiplying experiment yourself or imagine it. Why do you think a hard calculation makes you stop walking?
Kahneman says System 2 is lazy. Is that always a bad thing, or can laziness in thinking sometimes be the right choice?
hook: Kahneman has a cheeky way of explaining which part of your brain is really in charge — and it turns out the part that thinks it is the hero is often just along for the ride.
